title: Can the LA Olympics in 2028 be a catalyst for clean energy? | Wood Mackenzie
gist: A Wood Mackenzie Energy Gang podcast episode with LACI's Matt Petersen argues the 2028 LA Olympics is being used as a deadline to pull forward transportation electrification, clean freight and grid-flexibility investments that Los Angeles needs regardless of the Games, rather than one-off Olympic venue spending.

insights.claim: The Los Angeles Cleantech Incubator (LACI) has used the run-up to the 2028 Olympics as a convening point for public and private sector action on transportation electrification, clean energy deployment and infrastructure, according to LACI president and CEO Matt Petersen.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Frames the 2028 LA Olympics as a forcing mechanism accelerating clean-transport capex timelines in one of the largest US metro economies, relevant for investors tracking EV charging and clean freight infrastructure buildout in Southern California.

insights.claim: Transport will be the single biggest source of greenhouse gas emissions associated with the LA Olympic Games, given Los Angeles's car-dependent transport system, comparable in daily logistical scale to seven Super Bowls happening every day.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Identifies transport electrification and mode-shift as the primary lever for reducing the Games' carbon footprint, the area most likely to see accelerated investment ahead of 2028.

insights.claim: LACI's modelling suggests Los Angeles still needs greater bus and rail ridership, along with better first-mile and last-mile options such as e-bike share and EV car-share schemes, if it wants to hit its climate targets before 2028.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Points to specific under-invested transit modes (bus/rail ridership, micromobility) as gaps that public/private capital may target ahead of 2028.

themes.theme: Grid and freight infrastructure as the real legacy

insights.claim: Key enabling investments for LA 2028 include electrification of freight, build-out of charging depots, battery-backed fast charging, and experiments with flexible grid connections that can bring new infrastructure online faster than traditional grid upgrades allow.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Highlights flexible/behind-the-meter grid connection models as a solution being tested in the LA context too, echoing a broader industry pattern (also seen in AI data centre power strategies) of bypassing slow grid upgrade timelines.

insights.claim: Matt Petersen argues Los Angeles has a better shot than most Olympic host cities at leaving a lasting legacy because it is not building a wave of new permanent venues, and its most important investments (electric buses, cleaner freight, charging networks, transit improvements) are in systems the city needs regardless of the Games.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Pushes back on the typical skepticism that Olympic host cities overpromise transformative benefits, arguing LA's model of investing in pre-existing needs rather than new venues makes the legacy claim more credible.
insights.locator: The most important investments are in systems LA needs anyway section
